**Three-Tier Flutter Testing Strategy:**

| Tier | Scope | Speed | Tools | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Test** | Pure Dart functions, classes, repositories | **Very Fast** (< 1s per file) | `test` package, `flutter_test` | Validate business logic, data models, utilities |
| **Widget Test** | Single widget or widget tree | **Fast** (1–10s) | `flutter_test`, `WidgetTester`, `pumpWidget` | Verify UI rendering, taps, gestures, state |
| **Integration Test** | Full app on real device/emulator | **Slow** (60s+) | `integration_test` package | End-to-end user flow validation |

**Unit Test Example:**
```dart
import 'package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart';
import 'package:myapp/calculator.dart';

void main() {
  test('addition returns correct sum', () {
    expect(Calculator.add(2, 3), 5);
  });
}
```

**Widget Test Example:**
```dart
testWidgets('Button shows snackbar on tap', (tester) async {
  await tester.pumpWidget(MyApp());
  await tester.tap(find.byType(ElevatedButton));
  await tester.pump(); // animate frame
  expect(find.text('Clicked!'), findsOneWidget);
});
```

**Integration Test Example:**
```dart
import 'package:integration_test/integration_test.dart';

void main() {
  IntegrationTestWidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized();

  testWidgets('Full login flow', (tester) async {
    await tester.pumpWidget(MyApp());
    await tester.enterText(find.byKey(Key('email')), 'user@test.com');
    await tester.enterText(find.byKey(Key('password')), 'pass123');
    await tester.tap(find.byKey(Key('login-btn')));
    await tester.pumpAndSettle(Duration(seconds: 5));
    expect(find.text('Dashboard'), findsOneWidget);
  });
}
```

**Test Pyramid Principle:**
- Maximize **unit tests** (cheap, fast, isolated).
- Use **widget tests** for UI contracts.
- Reserve **integration tests** for critical user journeys (login, checkout, etc.).

#### Part (b) Solution

```yaml
name: Flutter CI Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30

    steps:
      - name: Checkout Repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Java
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: 'temurin'
          java-version: '17'

      - name: Setup Flutter
        uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with:
          flutter-version: '3.24.0'
          channel: stable

      - name: Cache Pub Dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.pub-cache
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pub-${{ hashFiles('**/pubspec.lock') }}
          restore-keys: |
            ${{ runner.os }}-pub-

      - name: Install Dependencies
        run: flutter pub get

      - name: Verify Formatting
        run: dart format --output=none --set-exit-if-changed .

      - name: Analyze Code
        run: flutter analyze

      - name: Run Unit and Widget Tests
        run: flutter test --coverage

      - name: Build Android APK
        run: flutter build apk --release

      - name: Build Android App Bundle
        run: flutter build appbundle --release

      - name: Upload APK Artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: release-apk
          path: build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk

      - name: Upload AAB Artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: release-aab
          path: build/app/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab

      - name: iOS Build (macOS Runner Only)
        if: runner.os == 'macOS'
        run: |
          cd ios
          pod install
          flutter build ios --release --no-codesign
```

**Explanation of Workflow:**

1. **Triggers (`on:`):** Runs on every push and PR to `main`.
2. **Runner:** `ubuntu-latest` for Android; iOS requires a `macos-latest` runner.
3. **Java 17:** Required for Android Gradle Plugin 8+.
4. **Flutter 3.24.0:** Pinned version for reproducibility.
5. **Cache:** Speeds up `flutter pub get` by caching `~/.pub-cache`.
6. **Format Check:** Enforces consistent code style.
7. **Analyze:** Static analysis with `dart analyze`.
8. **Test with Coverage:** Generates `lcov.info` for code coverage reporting.
9. **Build APK + AAB:** Produces both artifact types for distribution.
10. **Upload Artifacts:** Stores `.apk` and `.aab` for download from the Actions UI.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - Students frequently forget to **cache `~/.pub-cache`** — without it, every run downloads dependencies (~2–5 min wasted).
> - Missing `timeout-minutes` can cause workflows to hang indefinitely and consume CI minutes.
> - The `macos-latest` runner is **~10× more expensive** than `ubuntu-latest`; gate iOS builds with `if: runner.os == 'macOS'` to avoid Linux failure.
> - `--no-codesign` is required for CI builds without Apple certificates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### 🎯 Animation Concepts
- [ ] `AnimationController` produces normalized values in $[0.0, 1.0]$.
- [ ] Always call `_controller.dispose()` in `State.dispose()`.
- [ ] `vsync: this` requires `SingleTickerProviderStateMixin` (one controller) or `TickerProviderStateMixin` (multiple).
- [ ] Implicit animations: `AnimatedContainer`, `AnimatedOpacity`, `AnimatedAlign`.
- [ ] Explicit animations: `AnimationController` + `Tween` + `AnimatedBuilder`.
- [ ] Hero tags must be **unique per route**.
- [ ] `CurvedAnimation` wraps controller to apply easing curves.

### 🔒 Security Concepts
- [ ] Use `flutter_secure_storage` for tokens, NOT `SharedPreferences`.
- [ ] Always run `flutter build --obfuscate --split-debug-info=./symbols` for release.
- [ ] Certificate pinning prevents MITM attacks.
- [ ] JWT must be validated; reject `alg: "none"`.
- [ ] OWASP Mobile Top 10 is the industry security benchmark.
- [ ] HTTPS is mandatory; cleartext HTTP is blocked by default on iOS/Android.

### 🧪 Testing Concepts
- [ ] Three tiers: Unit → Widget → Integration.
- [ ] `pumpWidget()` mounts a widget tree in tests.
- [ ] `pump()` advances one frame; `pumpAndSettle()` advances until idle.
- [ ] `find.byKey()`, `find.text()`, `find.byType()` locate widgets.
- [ ] Target coverage: **≥ 80%** for production apps.
- [ ] `integration_test` runs on real devices for full E2E.

### 🚀 CI/CD Concepts
- [ ] Stages: **lint → test → build → sign → deploy**.
- [ ] GitHub Actions uses YAML in `.github/workflows/`.
- [ ] Android signing requires `.jks` keystore + `key.properties`.
- [ ] iOS signing requires `.p12` + Provisioning Profile.
- [ ] Cache `~/.pub-cache` to speed up CI.
- [ ] Use matrix builds for parallel testing across Flutter versions.

### 📱 Publishing Concepts
- [ ] Google Play: $25 one-time, AAB upload, staged rollout.
- [ ] Apple App Store: $99/year, IPA upload, manual review.
- [ ] Internal → Closed → Open → Production tracks.
- [ ] Privacy Policy URL is **mandatory** on both stores.
- [ ] Version codes (`versionCode` Android, `CFBundleVersion` iOS) must monotonically increase.

### 🌟 Industry Trends
- [ ] **Impeller** engine (Flutter 3.10+) is GPU-first.
- [ ] Flutter supports **mobile, web, desktop, embedded**.
- [ ] **WASM** is the future of Flutter Web performance.
- [ ] **Toyota** uses embedded Flutter for infotainment.
- [ ] **AI-assisted coding** is standard in modern dev workflows.
- [ ] **Foldable devices** require adaptive `MediaQuery` layouts.

### 📐 Key Formula Quick Recall

| Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V(t) = V_0 + (V_1 - V_0) \cdot f(t)$ | Animation interpolation |
| $\Delta t_{frame} = \frac{1000}{60}$ ms | 60 FPS frame budget |
| $T_{matrix} = \max_j \sum_i T_{step,i}$ | Parallel CI pipeline time |
| $E_{AES}(P, K) = C$ | Symmetric encryption |
| $S = \frac{T_{serial}}{T_{matrix}}$ | CI speedup factor |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignments/Projects: Add advanced UI components and animations to the project, Implement security measures in the Flutter application, Conduct thorough testing and debugging of the developed app
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST695 - MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Assignments/Projects: Add advanced UI components and animations to the project, Implement security measures in the Flutter application, Conduct thorough testing and debugging of the developed app

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Project Enhancements: Advanced UI, Security & Testing in Flutter

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What does this module mean in KTU 2024 Scheme context?

The **PECST695 – Mobile Application Development** project module under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** expects every student to evolve a *minimal viable Flutter app* into a **production-grade mobile application**. The three pillars of this evolution are:

| Pillar | What it really means | KTU Outcome Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Advanced UI & Animations** | Beyond `Container` and `Text` — using `Slivers`, `Hero`, `Implicit/Explicit Animations`, `CustomPainter`, `Lottie` | CO1, CO2 (Apply & Design) |
| **Security** | Protecting data at rest + data in transit + secure code practices (auth, encrypted storage, HTTPS, code obfuscation) | CO3 (Implement) |
| **Testing & Debugging** | Unit, Widget, Integration tests + DevTools, `flutter test`, breakpoints, logging | CO4 (Analyze & Test) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** This is a *Project-Based Module*. Marks are awarded in the **Internal Evaluation** (Continuous Assessment) and **Viva-Voce**. You will be judged on **working code, not theory alone**. Yet theory is essential for answering the 14-mark written components.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine your **Flutter app is a restaurant** 🍽️:

- **Advanced UI & Animations** = the **ambience, plating, and table service** of the restaurant. The food might be good, but the *experience* — smooth sliding menus, animated page transitions, shimmering buttons — is what keeps customers coming back.
- **Security** = the **locks, alarm system, CCTV, and food safety standards** of the restaurant. Even with great ambience, customers won't eat there if their data is leaked or their payment is insecure.
- **Testing & Debugging** = the **quality control kitchen, taste tests, and the head chef randomly sampling every dish** before it leaves the kitchen. A dish that looks beautiful but tastes bad is a *bug*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars in One Line:**
> - UI/Animations = **How it looks & feels**
> - Security = **How it protects**
> - Testing = **How it holds up under pressure**

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Glossary of Core Terms (KTU-Standard Definitions)**
>
> - **Widget Tree** — The hierarchical, immutable structure of UI elements in Flutter. Every visual component is a `Widget`.
> - **Implicit Animation** — Animations where Flutter automatically interpolates between values when they change (e.g., `AnimatedContainer`, `AnimatedOpacity`).
> - **Explicit Animation** — Animations you control manually using an `AnimationController` and `Ticker`.
> - **Hero Animation** — A special animation that flies an element from one route to another during navigation.
> - **CustomPainter** — Low-level drawing on a `Canvas` using `Paint` objects, similar to HTML5 canvas.
> - **Flutter Secure Storage** — A plugin providing **AES-encrypted** key-value storage backed by **iOS Keychain** and **Android Keystore**.
> - **HTTPS / TLS** — Transport Layer Security protocol ensuring encrypted communication between client and server. Flutter's `dio` and `http` packages support it.
> - **Code Obfuscation** — Using `flutter build --obfuscate --split-debug-info` to make reverse engineering harder.
> - **Unit Test** — Tests pure Dart functions in isolation. Runs via `flutter test`.
> - **Widget Test** — Tests a single widget in isolation using `WidgetTester`.
> - **Integration Test** — Tests the full app on a real device/emulator using `flutter_driver` or the newer `integration_test` package.
> - **Flutter DevTools** — A suite of debugging and profiling tools (widget inspector, timeline, memory profiler).

---

### 1.4 The Flutter Project Stack — At a Glance

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                YOUR FLUTTER APP                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  UI Layer    →  Widgets, Slivers, Animations     │
│  State Layer →  Provider / Riverpod / Bloc       │
│  Service     →  API calls, Local DB, Auth        │
│  Security    →  SecureStorage, HTTPS, Obfuscate  │
│  Tests       →  test/, integration_test/         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **default KTU project structure** (using `flutter create`) already has a `test/` folder pre-configured with one sample `widget_test.dart`. You are expected to *expand* this — not delete it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar A — Advanced UI Components

### 2.1.1 The Widget Tree Hierarchy

Flutter's UI is built entirely from **widgets**. Widgets fall into two broad categories:

$$
\text{Widgets} = \begin{cases} \text{StatelessWidget} & \text{(immutable, rebuilds on parent change)} \\ \text{StatefulWidget} & \text{(mutable via State, controlled rebuilds)} \end{cases}
$$

The **Element Tree** sits between the Widget Tree and the **RenderObject Tree**, performing *reconciliation* (the diffing algorithm) to update only what changed.

### 2.1.2 Advanced Widget Categories You MUST Use

| Category | KTU-Mandated Widgets | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Sliver Family** | `SliverAppBar`, `SliverList`, `SliverGrid` | Scrollable headers, collapsing toolbars |
| **Input & Form** | `TextField` with `InputDecoration`, `Form`, `FormField`, validators | Login, registration, search |
| **Navigation** | `Navigator`, `Named Routes`, `BottomNavigationBar`, `Drawer` | Multi-screen flow |
| **Feedback** | `SnackBar`, `AlertDialog`, `ModalBottomSheet`, `Tooltip` | User confirmation, errors |
| **Media** | `Image.network`, `Image.asset`, `CachedNetworkImage`, `Lottie` | Avatars, splash, animations |
| **Data Display** | `ListView.builder`, `GridView.builder`, `DataTable` | Long lists, catalogs |
| **Theming** | `ThemeData`, `ColorScheme`, `TextTheme` | Dark mode, brand consistency |

### 2.1.3 The Two Animation Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Implicit Animations** are *declarative* — *"Make this Container become red over 300ms when I tap it."*
> **Explicit Animations** are *imperative* — *"Run a controller from 0.0 → 1.0 over 2 seconds with a bounce curve, and rebuild 60 times per second."*

**Decision Rule for KTU Viva:**

$$
\text{Choose Implicit} \iff \text{simple state-driven transition} \quad \text{(e.g., color, size, opacity)}
$$

$$
\text{Choose Explicit} \iff \text{multi-step, curve-based, or repeating animation}
$$

### 2.1.4 Implicit Animation — The `AnimatedXxx` Family

The most common implicit widgets in a KTU project:

| Widget | Animated Property |
|---|---|
| `AnimatedContainer` | width, height, color, padding, decoration, transform |
| `AnimatedOpacity` | opacity |
| `AnimatedAlign` | alignment |
| `AnimatedPadding` | padding |
| `AnimatedPositioned` | top, left, right, bottom (inside a `Stack`) |
| `AnimatedSwitcher` | child widget swap with transition |
| `TweenAnimationBuilder` | arbitrary value (most flexible) |

### 2.1.5 Explicit Animation — The `AnimationController` Lifecycle

The full lifecycle of an `AnimationController`:

$$
\text{Create} \rightarrow \text{Initialize (vsync)} \rightarrow \text{forward/repeat} \rightarrow \text{Listener rebuilds} \rightarrow \text{dispose()}
$$

**Key Equation — Animation Duration vs. FPS:**

$$
\text{Total Frames} = \text{Duration (s)} \times \text{Frame Rate (fps)}
$$

A 2-second animation at 60 FPS produces **120 frames** of work for the GPU.

### 2.1.6 The Hero Animation

Heroes are tagged widgets that visually "fly" from one `Route` to another. The tag **must be unique** per source-destination pair.

**Flow:**

$$
\text{Screen A (tag="avatar")} \xrightarrow{\text{push}} \text{Screen B (same tag)} \Rightarrow \text{fly animation}
$$

### 2.1.7 CustomPainter — Pixel-Perfect Drawing

`CustomPainter` gives you a `Canvas` + `Paint` pair, similar to Android's `Canvas` or HTML5 `<canvas>`. It is the most performant way to draw custom shapes, charts, signatures, and progress rings.

**The `shouldRepaint` Decision:**

$$
\text{shouldRepaint} = \text{true} \iff \text{oldDelegate} \neq \text{newDelegate}
$$

Returning `true` triggers a full repaint — expensive. Always override `shouldRepaint` to return `true` only when fields actually changed.

---

## 2.2 Pillar B — Security in Flutter

### 2.2.1 The CIA Triad Applied to Mobile

The classical security model applies directly:

$$
\text{Security} = \text{Confidentiality} + \text{Integrity} + \text{Availability}
$$

| Principle | Mobile App Implementation |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Encrypt tokens, use HTTPS, secure storage |
| **Integrity** | Validate SSL certificates, sign requests with HMAC |
| **Availability** | Rate limiting, offline cache, error boundaries |

### 2.2.2 Threat Model for a Typical KTU Project

| Threat | Example | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping** | MITM on public Wi-Fi | Force HTTPS, certificate pinning |
| **Local Data Theft** | Rooted Android extracts SharedPreferences | Use `flutter_secure_storage` |
| **Reverse Engineering** | APK decompiled with `jadx` | `--obfuscate --split-debug-info` |
| **Token Theft** | JWT stored in plain text | Secure storage + auto-refresh |
| **Injection** | SQL injection via un-sanitized input | Parameterized queries (`sqflite`) |
| **Insecure Auth** | Weak password rules, no 2FA | Firebase Auth, regex validators |

### 2.2.3 The `flutter_secure_storage` Encryption Stack

On Android, the plugin uses **AES-256** with keys stored in the **Android Keystore** (hardware-backed on devices with a TEE).

$$
\text{Plaintext} \xrightarrow{\text{AES-256-GCM}} \text{Ciphertext} \xrightarrow{\text{Keystore (HW)}} \text{Safe Storage}
$$

On iOS, the plugin uses the **Keychain Services API**, which marks entries as `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly`.

### 2.2.4 The Dart `http` vs `dio` Decision

| Feature | `package:http` | `package:dio` |
|---|---|---|
| Basic GET/POST | ✅ | ✅ |
| Interceptors | ❌ | ✅ (token injection, logging) |
| Cancellation | ❌ | ✅ (CancelToken) |
| File upload progress | ❌ | ✅ (onSendProgress) |
| Certificate pinning | Manual | Via `badCertificateCallback` |

For KTU projects, **`dio` is recommended** because the interceptor pattern cleanly demonstrates security best practices (auto-attach JWT, refresh on 401, log safely).

### 2.2.5 Obfuscation Command (KTU Viva Gold)

```bash
flutter build apk --release \
  --obfuscate \
  --split-debug-info=./debug-info
```

This renames Dart symbols to meaningless tokens and splits the symbol map outside the APK.

---

## 2.3 Pillar C — Testing & Debugging

### 2.3.1 The Testing Pyramid

$$
\text{Unit Tests (base, fast, many)} \;\;\; < \;\;\; \text{Widget Tests (mid, medium)} \;\;\; < \;\;\; \text{Integration Tests (top, slow, few)}
$$

### 2.3.2 Test Type Comparison Table

| Test Type | What it tests | Tool/Command | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Pure Dart functions, business logic | `flutter test test/unit/` | ~ms |
| **Widget** | A widget renders correctly, taps work | `WidgetTester` + `pumpWidget` | ~100ms |
| **Integration** | The full app on a real device | `integration_test` package | ~seconds |
| **Golden** | Pixel-perfect UI snapshots | `matchesGoldenFile` | ~seconds |

### 2.3.3 The `flutter test` Lifecycle

$$
\text{Test File} \rightarrow \text{group()} \rightarrow \text{test()} \rightarrow \text{setUp} \rightarrow \text{expect()} \rightarrow \text{tearDown}
$$

### 2.3.4 Debugging Toolkit Matrix

| Tool | What it solves | How to open |
|---|---|---|
| **Print / `debugPrint`** | Quick state logging | Console output |
| **`developer.log()`** | Structured, filterable logs | Console / DevTools |
| **Breakpoints** | Pause and inspect variables | VS Code / Android Studio gutter |
| **Flutter DevTools** | Widget Inspector, Memory, Timeline, Network | `flutter run` then open DevTools |
| **Hot Reload (r)** | Update UI in < 1s | Save file or press `r` |
| **Hot Restart (R)** | Re-run `main()` preserving state | Press `R` |
| **Flutter Inspector** | Visualize widget tree, debug layout | DevTools tab |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Your **viva examiner** will almost certainly ask: *"How did you debug your project?"* Have one specific, real example ready — e.g., *"I used the Widget Inspector to find a `RenderFlex overflowed` error caused by an unbounded `Row`."*

### 2.3.5 Code Coverage Command

```bash
flutter test --coverage
genhtml coverage/lcov.info -o coverage/html
```

This generates an HTML report showing which lines are exercised by tests. **KTU projects with > 70% coverage earn bonus appreciation marks.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Pillar | Used in Industry For |
|---|---|
| Advanced UI | WhatsApp (Hero on contact pics), Airbnb (Lottie animations), Google Pay (CustomPainter for card animations) |
| Security | Banking apps (tokenization, secure storage), m-Commerce (certificate pinning) |
| Testing | Google's own Flutter team runs 100k+ tests per release; CI/CD via GitHub Actions + Codemagic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementations & Analytical Matrices

## 3.1 Pillar A — Building a Production-Grade UI Component

### 3.1.1 Implementation: A `SliverAppBar` with `SliverList` (Collapsing Header)

**File:** `lib/widgets/collapsing_header.dart`

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

class CollapsingHeaderScreen extends StatelessWidget {
  const CollapsingHeaderScreen({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      body: CustomScrollView(
        slivers: [
          // 1. The collapsing app bar
          SliverAppBar(
            expandedHeight: 220.0,
            floating: false,
            pinned: true,
            snap: false,
            flexibleSpace: FlexibleSpaceBar(
              title: const Text('KTU Project'),
              background: Image.network(
                'https://picsum.photos/800/400',
                fit: BoxFit.cover,
                color: Colors.black.withOpacity(0.4),
                colorBlendMode: BlendMode.darken,
              ),
            ),
          ),
          // 2. The scrollable list
          SliverList(
            delegate: SliverChildBuilderDelegate(
              (context, index) => ListTile(
                leading: CircleAvatar(child: Text('${index + 1}')),
                title: Text('Sliver Item $index'),
                subtitle: const Text('KTU 2024 Advanced UI'),
              ),
              childCount: 30,
            ),
          ),
        ],
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- **Step 1:** `CustomScrollView` is the *only* scrollable that accepts slivers.
- **Step 2:** `SliverAppBar` collapses from **220px** to the toolbar height (~56px) on scroll.
- **Step 3:** `flexibleSpace` provides the *expanded* content — here an image with a dark overlay.
- **Step 4:** `SliverList.builder` lazy-builds only visible items — memory-efficient.

### 3.1.2 Implementation: Hero Animation Between Two Screens

**Screen A (Source):**

```dart
Hero(
  tag: 'profile-avatar',
  child: CircleAvatar(
    radius: 30,
    backgroundImage: NetworkImage(user.avatarUrl),
  ),
)
```

**Screen B (Destination):**

```dart
Hero(
  tag: 'profile-avatar',  // MUST match
  child: CircleAvatar(
    radius: 100,
    backgroundImage: NetworkImage(user.avatarUrl),
  ),
)
```

**Navigation trigger:**

```dart
Navigator.push(
  context,
  MaterialPageRoute(builder: (_) => ProfileScreen(user: user)),
);
```

The Hero automatically flies from **30px radius → 100px radius** during the route transition.

---

### 3.1.3 Implementation: Implicit vs Explicit Animation Comparison

**Implicit (15 lines):**

```dart
class AnimatedCard extends StatefulWidget {
  const AnimatedCard({super.key});
  @override
  State<AnimatedCard> createState() => _AnimatedCardState();
}

class _AnimatedCardState extends State<AnimatedCard> {
  bool _expanded = false;

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return GestureDetector(
      onTap: () => setState(() => _expanded = !_expanded),
      child: AnimatedContainer(
        duration: const Duration(milliseconds: 350),
        curve: Curves.easeInOutCubic,
        width: _expanded ? 300 : 150,
        height: _expanded ? 200 : 100,
        decoration: BoxDecoration(
          color: _expanded ? Colors.teal : Colors.indigo,
          borderRadius: BorderRadius.circular(_expanded ? 24 : 8),
        ),
        child: const Center(
          child: Text('Tap me', style: TextStyle(color: Colors.white)),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

**Explicit (AnimationController-driven):**

```dart
class BouncingBall extends StatefulWidget {
  const BouncingBall({super.key});
  @override
  State<BouncingBall> createState() => _BouncingBallState();
}

class _BouncingBallState extends State<BouncingBall>
    with SingleTickerProviderStateMixin {
  late final AnimationController _ctrl;
  late final Animation<double> _bounce;

  @override
  void initState() {
    super.initState();
    _ctrl = AnimationController(
      vsync: this,
      duration: const Duration(seconds: 2),
    )..repeat(reverse: true);

    _bounce = Tween<double>(begin: 0, end: 200).animate(
      CurvedAnimation(parent: _ctrl, curve: Curves.elasticOut),
    );
  }

  @override
  void dispose() {
    _ctrl.dispose();   // CRITICAL — prevents memory leak
    super.dispose();
  }

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return AnimatedBuilder(
      animation: _bounce,
      builder: (_, __) => Container(
        margin: EdgeInsets.only(top: _bounce.value),
        width: 60,
        height: 60,
        decoration: const BoxDecoration(
          color: Colors.orange,
          shape: BoxShape.circle,
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Aspect | Implicit | Explicit |
|---|---|---|
| Lines of code | ~15 | ~30 |
| Control | Limited (built-in curves) | Full (any curve, sequencing) |
| State machine | None | `AnimationStatus` listeners |
| Memory cleanup | Automatic | MUST call `dispose()` |
| Use case | Toggles, color fades, size pulses | Loading spinners, complex multi-step transitions |

---

### 3.1.4 Implementation: `CustomPainter` Progress Ring

```dart
class ProgressRing extends StatelessWidget {
  final double progress;   // 0.0 → 1.0
  const ProgressRing({super.key, required this.progress});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return SizedBox(
      width: 120,
      height: 120,
      child: CustomPaint(
        painter: _RingPainter(progress),
        child: Center(
          child: Text('${(progress * 100).toInt()}%'),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

class _RingPainter extends CustomPainter {
  final double progress;
  _RingPainter(this.progress);

  @override
  void paint(Canvas canvas, Size size) {
    final center = Offset(size.width / 2, size.height / 2);
    final radius = size.width / 2 - 8;

    // 1. Background ring
    final bgPaint = Paint()
      ..color = Colors.grey.shade300
      ..style = PaintingStyle.stroke
      ..strokeWidth = 8
      ..strokeCap = StrokeCap.round;
    canvas.drawCircle(center, radius, bgPaint);

    // 2. Foreground arc
    final fgPaint = Paint()
      ..color = Colors.teal
      ..style = PaintingStyle.stroke
      ..strokeWidth = 8
      ..strokeCap = StrokeCap.round;
    canvas.drawArc(
      Rect.fromCircle(center: center, radius: radius),
      -1.5708,                                       // -90° (start at top)
      progress * 6.2832,                              // 2π × progress
      false,
      fgPaint,
    );
  }

  @override
  bool shouldRepaint(_RingPainter old) => old.progress != progress;
}
```

**Key math behind the arc:**

$$
\theta_{\text{start}} = -\frac{\pi}{2} \quad \text{(top, 12 o'clock)}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{sweep}} = 2\pi \times \text{progress} \quad \text{(progress in [0, 1])}
$$

## 3.2 Pillar B — Security Implementation Walkthrough

### 3.2.1 Implementation: Secure Token Storage

**Add dependency (`pubspec.yaml`):**

```yaml
dependencies:
  flutter_secure_storage: ^9.2.2
```

**Secure storage service:**

```dart
import 'package:flutter_secure_storage/flutter_secure_storage.dart';

class TokenStore {
  static const _storage = FlutterSecureStorage(
    aOptions: AndroidOptions(encryptedSharedPreferences: true),
    iOptions: IOSOptions(
      accessibility: KeychainAccessibility.first_unlock,
    ),
  );

  static const _kAccessToken = 'access_token';
  static const _kRefreshToken = 'refresh_token';

  Future<void> saveTokens(String access, String refresh) async {
    await _storage.write(key: _kAccessToken, value: access);
    await _storage.write(key: _kRefreshToken, value: refresh);
  }

  Future<String?> readAccess()  => _storage.read(key: _kAccessToken);
  Future<String?> readRefresh() => _storage.read(key: _kRefreshToken);

  Future<void> clear() async {
    await _storage.deleteAll();
  }
}
```

### 3.2.2 Implementation: Dio with JWT Interceptor

```dart
import 'package:dio/dio.dart';
import 'token_store.dart';

class ApiClient {
  final Dio _dio;
  final TokenStore _store;

  ApiClient._(this._dio, this._store);

  factory ApiClient.create() {
    final dio = Dio(BaseOptions(
      baseUrl: 'https://api.example.com',
      connectTimeout: const Duration(seconds: 10),
      receiveTimeout: const Duration(seconds: 15),
    ));

    final store = TokenStore();
    final client = ApiClient._(dio, store);

    dio.interceptors.add(InterceptorsWrapper(
      onRequest: (options, handler) async {
        final token = await store.readAccess();
        if (token != null) {
          options.headers['Authorization'] = 'Bearer $token';
        }
        handler.next(options);
      },
      onError: (err, handler) async {
        if (err.response?.statusCode == 401) {
          // 1. Try refresh
          final refresh = await store.readRefresh();
          if (refresh != null) {
            // 2. Call /refresh endpoint, save new tokens
            // 3. Retry the original request
          }
        }
        handler.next(err);
      },
    ));
    return client;
  }
}
```

### 3.2.3 Implementation: Certificate Pinning

```dart
class _PinningInterceptor extends Interceptor {
  @override
  void onRequest(RequestOptions options, RequestInterceptorHandler handler) {
    // Forces connection only if cert hash matches
    (options.httpClient as IOClient).innerClient?.badCertificateCallback =
        (cert, host, port) => false;
    handler.next(options);
  }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For real certificate pinning, use the `dio_certificate_pinning` plugin or a custom `SecurityContext` — never pin without a fallback strategy.

### 3.3.5 Debugging Workflow — Step-by-Step

**Scenario:** "My app crashes on launch in release mode."

| Step | Action | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Check the stack trace | `flutter run --release` console |
| 2 | Look for `RenderFlex overflowed` | DevTools → Widget Inspector |
| 3 | Inspect widget constraints | DevTools → "Show Guidelines" |
| 4 | Add a `try/catch` + `FlutterError.onError` | Logger |
| 5 | Use `debugPrint` for runtime state | Console |
| 6 | Profile for jank | DevTools → Timeline |

**Step-by-step debugging code (global error handler):**

```dart
void main() {
  FlutterError.onError = (details) {
    FlutterError.presentError(details);
    debugPrint('=== FLUTTER ERROR ===');
    debugPrint('Exception: ${details.exception}');
    debugPrint('Library:   ${details.library}');
    debugPrint('Context:   ${details.context}');
  };

  PlatformDispatcher.instance.onError = (error, stack) {
    debugPrint('=== UNCAUGHT ERROR ===');
    debugPrint('Error: $error');
    debugPrint('Stack: $stack');
    return true;     // Mark as handled
  };

  runApp(const MyApp());
}
```

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow — Advanced UI / Animation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Interaction] --> B[GestureDetector / InkWell]
    B --> C{Animation Type?}
    C -- Implicit --> D[AnimatedContainer / AnimatedOpacity]
    C -- Explicit --> E[AnimationController]
    C -- Hero --> F[Hero Widget on Two Routes]
    D --> G[Widget Tree Rebuild]
    E --> H[Ticker Triggers 60Hz Callback]
    H --> I[AnimatedBuilder / setState]
    F --> J[Navigator.push]
    J --> K[Flight Animation Overlay]
    G --> L[RenderObject Tree]
    I --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[GPU Compositor]
    M --> N[Frame on Screen]
    L --> O[CustomPainter]
    O --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- A tap event branches into **three animation pathways** depending on the widget chosen.
- The **RenderObject Tree** is the single bottleneck where all visual updates converge.
- `CustomPainter` is a direct call into the **GPU Compositor**, bypassing the normal widget pipeline for pixel-perfect drawing.

---

## 4.2 Security Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientApp[Flutter App]
        UI[UI Screens]
        Auth[Auth Module]
        TokenStore[Secure Token Store]
        ApiClient[Dio API Client]
    end

    subgraph MobilePlatform[Mobile OS]
        Keystore[Android Keystore / iOS Keychain]
        TPM[Hardware TEE / Secure Enclave]
    end

    subgraph Network[Network Layer]
        TLS[TLS 1.2 / 1.3 Handshake]
        Cert[SSL Certificate Validation]
    end

    subgraph Backend[Remote Server]
        API[REST API]
        AuthSrv[Auth Service]
    end

    UI --> Auth
    Auth --> TokenStore
    TokenStore <--> Keystore
    Keystore <--> TPM
    Auth --> ApiClient
    ApiClient -->|HTTPS| TLS
    TLS --> Cert
    Cert --> API
    API --> AuthSrv
    AuthSrv -->|JWT| ApiClient
    ApiClient -->|Stores| TokenStore
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Secure Token Store** never writes plaintext — it delegates to the OS keystore.
- The keystore can leverage **hardware-backed encryption** (TEE) on supported devices.
- Network traffic is encrypted end-to-end with **TLS**, and the server returns a signed **JWT**.

---

## 4.3 Testing & Debugging Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Write Source Code] --> B[flutter analyze]
    B --> C{Issues?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Fix with DevTools Inspector]
    C -- No --> E[Run Unit Tests]
    D --> E
    E --> F{All Pass?}
    F -- No --> G[Debug with Print / Breakpoints]
    F -- Yes --> H[Run Widget Tests]
    G --> H
    H --> I{All Pass?}
    I -- No --> D
    I -- Yes --> J[Run Integration Tests on Device]
    J --> K{All Pass?}
    K -- No --> D
    K -- Yes --> L[Generate Coverage Report]
    L --> M{Coverage >= 70%?}
    M -- No --> N[Add More Tests]
    M -- Yes --> O[Build Release APK]
    O --> P[Obfuscate Code]
    P --> Q[Deploy]
    N --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- This is a **strict CI-style feedback loop**. Code can only proceed to release if all three test tiers pass.
- The **coverage gate (70%)** forces the developer to write meaningful tests, not just smoke tests.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between `StatelessWidget` and `StatefulWidget` in Flutter. When would you choose one over the other?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **StatelessWidget** — immutable; rebuilt only when parent passes new data. No internal mutable state. Used for static UI like labels, icons, dividers. (1 mark)
> - **StatefulWidget** — creates a `State<T>` object holding mutable data; can call `setState()` to trigger rebuilds. Used for forms, animations, toggles, counters. (1 mark)
> - **Rule of thumb:** *"If the widget changes in response to user input, time, or network data, use StatefulWidget. Otherwise, use StatelessWidget."* (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the role of `flutter_secure_storage`? Why is it preferred over `SharedPreferences` for sensitive data?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - `flutter_secure_storage` provides **AES-256 encrypted** key-value storage backed by platform-native secure enclaves — **Android Keystore** and **iOS Keychain**. (1 mark)
> - It protects data even on rooted/jailbroken devices by leveraging hardware-backed encryption where available. (1 mark)
> - `SharedPreferences` stores values as **plain XML** in the app's private directory, which is trivially extractable on a rooted device. Therefore, it is unsafe for tokens, passwords, or PII. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Advanced UI & Animations

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Implicit** and **Explicit** animations in Flutter. With a code snippet, demonstrate how to animate a `Container` from size 100×100 to 300×300 over 500ms using **both** approaches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a `CustomPainter`? Write a complete Dart implementation of a `CustomPainter` that draws a **gradient circular progress ring** with the percentage value in the center. Explain the role of `shouldRepaint`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Conceptual Difference (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Implicit | Explicit |
|---|---|---|
| Control | Automatic — Flutter interpolates | Manual — you drive the controller |
| Boilerplate | Low (no controller) | High (controller, ticker, dispose) |
| API surface | `AnimatedContainer`, `AnimatedOpacity`, `TweenAnimationBuilder` | `AnimationController`, `Tween`, `CurvedAnimation` |
| Best for | Simple value-driven transitions | Multi-step, repeating, or curve-rich animations |

**Implicit Code (2 marks):**

```dart
class ImplicitBox extends StatefulWidget {
  const ImplicitBox({super.key});
  @override
  State<ImplicitBox> createState() => _ImplicitBoxState();
}

class _ImplicitBoxState extends State<ImplicitBox> {
  bool _big = false;
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return GestureDetector(
      onTap: () => setState(() => _big = !_big),
      child: AnimatedContainer(
        duration: const Duration(milliseconds: 500),
        curve: Curves.easeInOut,
        width:  _big ? 300 : 100,
        height: _big ? 300 : 100,
        color: Colors.indigo,
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

**Explicit Code (2 marks):**

```dart
class ExplicitBox extends StatefulWidget {
  const ExplicitBox({super.key});
  @override
  State<ExplicitBox> createState() => _ExplicitBoxState();
}

class _ExplicitBoxState extends State<ExplicitBox>
    with SingleTickerProviderStateMixin {
  late final AnimationController _ctrl;
  late final Animation<double> _size;

  @override
  void initState() {
    super.initState();
    _ctrl = AnimationController(
      vsync: this,
      duration: const Duration(milliseconds: 500),
    );
    _size = Tween<double>(begin: 100, end: 300)
        .animate(CurvedAnimation(parent: _ctrl, curve: Curves.easeInOut));
  }

  @override
  void dispose() { _ctrl.dispose(); super.dispose(); }

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return GestureDetector(
      onTap: () => _big ? _ctrl.reverse() : _ctrl.forward(),
      child: AnimatedBuilder(
        animation: _size,
        builder: (_, __) => Container(
          width: _size.value, height: _size.value, color: Colors.teal,
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
`CustomPainter` is a class that lets you draw arbitrary shapes, paths, and text on a `Canvas`. It is invoked by a `CustomPaint` widget. The two critical methods are:

- `paint(Canvas canvas, Size size)` — *the actual drawing code*
- `shouldRepaint(CustomPainter oldDelegate)` — *tells Flutter whether to repaint*

**Implementation (4 marks):**

```dart
class GradientRingPainter extends CustomPainter {
  final double progress; // 0.0 to 1.0
  GradientRingPainter(this.progress);

  @override
  void paint(Canvas canvas, Size size) {
    final center = Offset(size.width / 2, size.height / 2);
    final radius = math.min(size.width, size.height) / 2 - 12;

    // 1. Background ring
    final bgPaint = Paint()
      ..color = Colors.grey.shade300
      ..style = PaintingStyle.stroke
      ..strokeWidth = 12
      ..strokeCap = StrokeCap.round;
    canvas.drawCircle(center, radius, bgPaint);

    // 2. Gradient sweep
    final rect = Rect.fromCircle(center: center, radius: radius);
    final gradient = SweepGradient(
      startAngle: -math.pi / 2,
      endAngle: 3 * math.pi / 2,
      colors: const [Colors.purple, Colors.blue, Colors.green, Colors.purple],
    );
    final fgPaint = Paint()
      ..shader = gradient.createShader(rect)
      ..style = PaintingStyle.stroke
      ..strokeWidth = 12
      ..strokeCap = StrokeCap.round;
    canvas.drawArc(rect, -math.pi / 2, 2 * math.pi * progress, false, fgPaint);

    // 3. Percentage text
    final tp = TextPainter(
      text: TextSpan(
        text: '${(progress * 100).toInt()}%',
        style: const TextStyle(
          fontSize: 28, fontWeight: FontWeight.bold, color: Colors.black87,
        ),
      ),
      textDirection: TextDirection.ltr,
    )..layout();
    tp.paint(canvas, center - Offset(tp.width / 2, tp.height / 2));
  }

  @override
  bool shouldRepaint(GradientRingPainter old) => old.progress != progress;
}
```

**`shouldRepaint` Explanation (1 mark):**
Returns `true` only when the `progress` value has changed. This prevents **unnecessary repaints**, which are expensive. If you always return `true`, you waste GPU cycles and risk jank.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Security & Testing

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe **three security threats** common to Flutter mobile applications. For each, explain the corresponding mitigation strategy with a code/config example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three tiers of testing** in Flutter with appropriate use cases. Write a sample **widget test** for a `LoginForm` widget that verifies: (i) email field exists, (ii) tapping "Login" without credentials shows an error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Threat 1 — Plain-text Token Storage (2.5 marks)**
- *Threat:* Storing JWT or OAuth tokens in `SharedPreferences` exposes them to any attacker who gains file-system access (rooted Android, jailbroken iOS, malicious backup extractor).
- *Mitigation:* Use `flutter_secure_storage`, which encrypts values with AES-256 and stores keys in the Android Keystore / iOS Keychain.
- *Code:*

```dart
final storage = const FlutterSecureStorage();
await storage.write(key: 'jwt', value: token);   // Encrypted
final token = await storage.read(key: 'jwt');     // Decrypted on read
```

**Threat 2 — Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack (2.5 marks)**
- *Threat:* An attacker on the same Wi-Fi network intercepts unencrypted HTTP traffic.
- *Mitigation:* Force HTTPS, validate SSL certificates, optionally pin the server's certificate.
- *Config in `dio`:*

```dart
final dio = Dio(BaseOptions(baseUrl: 'https://api.ktuapp.com'));
// Reject any invalid certificate (default behavior — never override in prod)
dio.httpClientAdapter = IOHttpClientAdapter();
```

For build, add to `AndroidManifest.xml`:
```xml
android:usesCleartextTraffic="false"
```

**Threat 3 — Reverse Engineering (2 marks)**
- *Threat:* APK decompilation with `jadx` reveals business logic, API keys, and class names.
- *Mitigation:* Use the `--obfuscate` flag at build time.

```bash
flutter build apk --release --obfuscate --split-debug-info=./symbols
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Three Testing Tiers (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Unit} \subset \text{Widget} \subset \text{Integration}
$$

| Tier | Tests | Speed | Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Pure Dart functions (e.g., `Calculator.add`) | Fast (~ms) | `flutter test` |
| **Widget** | A widget's render & interaction in isolation | Medium (~100ms) | `WidgetTester` |
| **Integration** | Full app flow on a real device | Slow (~s) | `integration_test` |

**Use case:** Unit for business logic, Widget for UI components, Integration for end-to-end user flows.

**Sample Widget Test (4 marks):**

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
import 'package:flutter_test/flutter_test.dart';
import 'package:myapp/screens/login_form.dart';

void main() {
  testWidgets('Login form shows validation errors when fields are empty',
      (WidgetTester tester) async {
    // 1. Pump the widget
    await tester.pumpWidget(const MaterialApp(home: LoginForm()));
    await tester.pumpAndSettle();

    // 2. (i) Verify email field exists
    expect(find.byKey(const Key('email_field')), findsOneWidget);

    // 3. Tap the Login button without entering credentials
    await tester.tap(find.byKey(const Key('login_button')));
    await tester.pump();      // Trigger the snackbar/error widget

    // 4. (ii) Verify an error message is displayed
    expect(find.text('Email is required'), findsOneWidget);
    expect(find.text('Password is required'), findsOneWidget);
  });
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `_ctrl.dispose()`** in explicit animations — **2 marks lost** for memory leak explanation.
> 2. **Storing JWT in `SharedPreferences`** without explaining `flutter_secure_storage` — **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Using `print()` instead of `debugPrint()`** in production code — **0.5 mark lost** (debugPrint is throttled and stripped in release).
> 4. **Confusing `setState` rebuild with `Provider` rebuild** — in widget tests, always call `tester.pump()` after `setState` to flush the frame.
> 5. **Not overriding `shouldRepaint`** — examiners specifically look for this; returning `true` always is acceptable in code but explaining the perf cost earns full marks.
> 6. **No `Key` on testable widgets** — without `Key('email_field')`, `find.byKey()` returns nothing and the test fails for the wrong reason.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Project Enhancements**

### A. Advanced UI
- [ ] Know the **sliver family** — `SliverAppBar`, `SliverList`, `SliverGrid`
- [ ] Difference: `StatelessWidget` vs `StatefulWidget`
- [ ] `Hero` requires the **same `tag` on both routes**
- [ ] Implicit animations need **only a state change**
- [ ] Explicit animations require `vsync`, `forward/repeat`, and **`dispose()`**
- [ ] `CustomPainter` MUST override `shouldRepaint` for performance
- [ ] `TweenAnimationBuilder` is the **bridge** between implicit and explicit

### B. Security
- [ ] **Never** use `SharedPreferences` for tokens, passwords, or PII
- [ ] `flutter_secure_storage` → **Android Keystore** + **iOS Keychain**
- [ ] Always enforce **HTTPS**; set `usesCleartextTraffic="false"`
- [ ] Use **interceptors** in `dio` to attach `Bearer` tokens
- [ ] Build release with `--obfuscate --split-debug-info`
- [ ] Implement **global error handlers** with `FlutterError.onError`
- [ ] The **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the *correct theoretical framework* for viva questions

### C. Testing & Debugging
- [ ] **Unit → Widget → Integration** is the testing pyramid
- [ ] Use `find.byKey()` for testable widgets — always add `Key`s
- [ ] `tester.pump()` after `setState`; `tester.pumpAndSettle()` after async
- [ ] Code coverage target: **≥ 70%**
- [ ] Debugging tools: `debugPrint`, breakpoints, **DevTools**, Widget Inspector
- [ ] Hot Reload (`r`) ≠ Hot Restart (`R`); know the difference
- [ ] Always wrap `runApp` with a **global error handler** for production

### D. Project Viva Gold Phrases
- *"I used `flutter_secure_storage` because SharedPreferences stores plaintext XML, which is trivially extractable on a rooted device."*
- *"I implemented an `AnimationController` with `SingleTickerProviderStateMixin` and disposed it in `dispose()` to prevent memory leaks."*
- *"My widget test uses `tester.pumpAndSettle()` because the login flow involves a `Future` that completes asynchronously."*
- *"I built the release APK with `--obfuscate --split-debug-info` to make reverse engineering harder while keeping the symbol map for crash reporting."*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST695 - MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Milestone 4: Complete the project, integrating all features and preparing it for deployment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Project-Based Learning (PBL)** framework for Mobile Application Development, **Milestone 4** represents the **Final Integration, Stabilization, and Pre-Deployment Phase** of the software development life cycle. It is the convergence stage where independently developed modules (UI, business logic, database, APIs, authentication, notifications, etc.) are unified into a single, cohesive, production-grade Android/iOS/Flutter/React Native application, validated through end-to-end testing, optimized for performance, and packaged into a release-ready artifact (e.g., `.apk`, `.aab`, `.ipa`) suitable for distribution via the Google Play Store, Apple App Store, or enterprise channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Milestone 4 is the capstone phase that transforms a *functional prototype* into a *market-ready product*. It is assessed for **integration completeness**, **non-functional compliance** (security, performance, accessibility), and **deployment readiness** (signed binaries, store metadata, rollback strategy).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **fully furnished house**:
- **Milestones 1–3** were the construction phases: laying the foundation, building walls, installing plumbing and electrical wiring room-by-room.
- **Milestone 4** is the **"Move-In Day"** phase. You must:
  1. **Connect the plumbing to every bathroom** (Integration).
  2. **Test every switch, tap, and door** (End-to-End Testing).
  3. **Paint, polish, and stage the house** (UI/UX Polish & Optimization).
  4. **Get the occupancy certificate and register the address** (Deployment & Store Listing).

In a mobile app, "move-in" means the app works flawlessly when a real user installs it on a real device with real network conditions.

### 1.3 Core Constituents of Milestone 4

| Constituent | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Feature Integration** | Merging all branches/modules into the `main` branch via CI |
| **Regression Testing** | Ensuring old features still work after new integrations |
| **Performance Profiling** | Reducing APK size, memory leaks, cold-start time |
| **Security Hardening** | Obfuscation, certificate pinning, secure storage |
| **Release Engineering** | Signing, versioning, generating store-ready artifacts |
| **Deployment Automation** | CI/CD pipelines, staged rollouts, crash monitoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly evaluates whether the student can articulate *why* a particular build tool (Gradle/Xcode) or *why* a particular deployment strategy (Internal Testing → Closed Beta → Production) was chosen. Generic "I built the app" answers score poorly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Milestone 4

#### Pillar 1: **Continuous Integration (CI)**
Every code commit triggers an **automated build + test pipeline** to detect integration conflicts early.

#### Pillar 2: **Continuous Delivery / Deployment (CD)**
The integrated codebase is automatically packaged, signed, and pushed to a staging environment (e.g., Firebase App Distribution, TestFlight).

#### Pillar 3: **Quality Assurance (QA) & Acceptance**
A mix of **Unit Tests**, **Widget Tests** (Flutter), **UI Tests** (Espresso/XCUITest), and **Manual Exploratory Testing**.

#### Pillar 4: **Release Engineering & Telemetry**
App signing, version bumping, ProGuard/R8 minification, and integrating **Firebase Crashlytics** / **Sentry** for post-release monitoring.

### 2.2 The Git Branching Strategy (Why It Matters in Milestone 4)

```
main (production) ← stable, signed, deployable
  └── release/v1.0.0 ← release candidate branch
        └── develop ← integration branch
              ├── feature/auth
              ├── feature/payment
              └── feature/notifications
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Students must demonstrate a **Merge / Rebase / Squash** strategy and justify the choice. The 2024 Scheme CO ("Implement industry-standard version control workflows") is tested here.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table

| Metric | Formula / Standard | Target Value (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| **APK Size** | $\text{Size} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Module}_i + \text{Resources}$ | $\le 50\text{ MB}$ (ideal) |
| **Cold Start Time** | $T_{\text{cold}} = T_{\text{class load}} + T_{\text{inflate}} + T_{\text{render}}$ | $\le 2000\text{ ms}$ (Android Vitals) |
| **Crash-Free Users** | $\text{CFR} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Crashed Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}}\right) \times 100\%$ | $\ge 99.5\%$ |
| **Code Coverage** | $\text{Cov} = \frac{\text{LC}_{\text{executed}}}{\text{LC}_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | $\ge 70\%$ (industry) |
| **ANR Rate** | $\text{ANR}_{\text{rate}} = \frac{\text{ANR events}}{\text{Daily sessions}}$ | $\le 0.47\%$ (Google threshold) |
| **Version Code** | $\text{versionCode}_{n+1} = \text{versionCode}_{n} + 1$ | Monotonically increasing integer |
| **Semantic Version** | $\text{MAJOR}.\text{MINOR}.\text{PATCH}$ | e.g., 1.4.2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Constraint Check:** The markdown table above uses `\vert` separators implicitly through standard pipes. For inline math like absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ to prevent breaking the table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Application of Milestone 4 |
|---|---|
| **FinTech (e.g., Paytm, PhonePe)** | PCI-DSS compliance, certificate pinning, obfuscation |
| **Healthcare (e.g., Practo)** | HIPAA-compliant encryption, audit trails, staged rollouts |
| **E-Commerce (e.g., Flipkart)** | A/B testing via Firebase Remote Config, feature flags |
| **IoT / Smart Home** | OTA (Over-The-Air) update channel via Play Store or custom MDM |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Milestone 4 Execution Workflow

The following analytical breakdown follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme PBL Rubric** for the final project submission.

#### **Phase 1: Codebase Integration & Branch Unification**

**Step 1.1** — Verify all feature branches are merged into `develop`.

$$
\text{Merge Status} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}_{\text{merged}}(\text{branch}_i)
$$

Where $\mathbb{1}_{\text{merged}}$ is an indicator function returning 1 if branch $i$ is merged, 0 otherwise. A score of 1 means full integration.

**Step 1.2** — Resolve merge conflicts using a defined policy:
- **Whose Lines Win:** Senior dev / lead integrator decides.
- **Tool-assisted:** Git's `merge.tool` (e.g., `meld`, `vscode`).

**Step 1.3** — Run a **full regression suite**:
$$
T_{\text{regression}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{unit},i} + \sum_{j=1}^{m} T_{\text{widget},j} + T_{\text{E2E}}
$$

**Step 1.4** — Tag the release candidate:
```
git tag -a v1.0.0-rc.1 -m "Release Candidate 1"
git push origin v1.0.0-rc.1
```

---

#### **Phase 2: Performance Profiling & Optimization**

**Step 2.1** — Measure cold start time using Android Studio Profiler:

$$
T_{\text{cold}} = T_{\text{zygote fork}} + T_{\text{App init}} + T_{\text{First frame}}
$$

**Step 2.2** — Apply optimizations:
- **R8 / ProGuard** → Code shrinking and obfuscation.
- **AAPT2** → Resource shrinking.
- **Baseline Profiles** → Pre-compile hot code paths.

**Step 2.3** — Verify the post-optimization APK size:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{optimized}} = \text{Size}_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \text{shrinkage ratio})
$$

For a typical 30 MB raw APK with 35% shrinkage, $\text{Size}_{\text{optimized}} \approx 19.5\text{ MB}$.

---

#### **Phase 3: Security Hardening**

**Step 3.1** — Enable code obfuscation in `build.gradle`:

```gradle
buildTypes {
    release {
        minifyEnabled true
        shrinkResources true
        proguardFiles getDefaultProguardFile('proguard-android-optimize.txt'), 'proguard-rules.pro'
    }
}
```

**Step 3.2** — Configure Network Security Config for HTTPS-only traffic:

```xml
<network-security-config>
    <base-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="false">
        <trust-anchors>
            <certificates src="system" />
        </trust-anchors>
    </base-config>
</network-security-config>
```

**Step 3.3** — Use the Android Keystore for signing:

$$
\text{Signature} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{APK contents} \oplus \text{Keystore private key})
$$

---

#### **Phase 4: Release Engineering & Build Generation**

**Step 4.1** — Bump the version:
- `versionCode`: Integer (e.g., 1 → 2).
- `versionName`: Semantic string (e.g., "1.0.0" → "1.1.0").

**Step 4.2** — Generate the signed Android App Bundle (`.aab`):

$$
\text{Artifact} = \text{Sign}(\text{Build}(\text{Source}), \text{Keystore})
$$

**Step 4.3** — Generate the iOS archive (`.ipa`) via Xcode Organizer (if cross-platform).

---

#### **Phase 5: Deployment & Store Submission**

**Step 5.1** — **Internal Testing** (Firebase App Distribution / TestFlight):
$$
\text{Deployment}_{\text{internal}} = \{\text{Testers} : \text{role} = \text{QA} \cup \text{Stakeholders}\}
$$

**Step 5.2** — **Closed Beta** (10–1000 users via Play Console tracks).

**Step 5.3** — **Production Rollout** with staged release:
$$
\text{Rollout}\%_{t} = \min\left(100, \text{Rollout}\%_{t-1} \times r\right)
$$

Where $r$ is the rollout multiplier (commonly 2x or 1.5x per day).

**Step 5.4** — **Post-Launch Monitoring:** Enable Crashlytics and Performance Monitoring.

### 3.2 KTU PBL Assessment Matrix (How Your Project Will Be Graded)

| Evaluation Criterion | Weightage | KTU CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Feature Completeness | **20%** | CO1, CO2 |
| Integration Stability (No merge conflicts, clean build) | **15%** | CO3 |
| Test Coverage & QA Report | **15%** | CO4 |
| Performance Metrics (APK size, startup time) | **10%** | CO5 |
| Security Implementation | **10%** | CO5 |
| Deployment Artifacts (signed AAB/IPA) | **15%** | CO6 |
| Documentation & Viva | **15%** | CO6 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Milestone 4 End-to-End Pipeline (CI/CD Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Commit] --> B[Git Push to develop branch]
    B --> C{CI Pipeline Triggered}
    C --> D[Compile and Lint]
    D --> E[Run Unit Tests]
    E --> F{Tests Passed?}
    F -- No --> G[Notify Developer via Slack or Email]
    G --> A
    F -- Yes --> H[Build Debug APK]
    H --> I[Run Widget and UI Tests on Emulator]
    I --> J{All Tests Green?}
    J -- No --> G
    J -- Yes --> K[Merge to main branch]
    K --> L[Tag Release Candidate v1.0.0]
    L --> M[Build Release AAB with R8]
    M --> N[Sign with Keystore]
    N --> O[Upload to Firebase App Distribution]
    O --> P[Internal QA Testing]
    P --> Q{QA Approved?}
    Q -- No --> R[Create Hotfix Branch]
    R --> A
    Q -- Yes --> S[Submit to Play Console Internal Track]
    S --> T[Closed Beta with 100 Testers]
    T --> U[Staged Production Rollout 10 percent 50 percent 100 percent]
    U --> V[Monitor Crashlytics and ANR Dashboard]
    V --> W{Issue Detected?}
    W -- Yes --> X[Rollback via Play Console]
    X --> R
    W -- No --> Y[Release Stable]
```

### 4.2 Integration Testing Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UI_Layer [UI Layer]
        UI1[Login Screen]
        UI2[Dashboard Screen]
        UI3[Profile Screen]
        UI4[Settings Screen]
    end

    subgraph Logic_Layer [Business Logic Layer]
        L1[Auth Controller]
        L2[Data Repository]
        L3[Notification Service]
        L4[Payment Gateway Handler]
    end

    subgraph Data_Layer [Data Layer]
        D1[Firebase Firestore]
        D2[Local SQLite or Room]
        D3[SharedPreferences or SecureStore]
        D4[Remote REST API]
    end

    UI1 --> L1
    UI2 --> L2
    UI3 --> L2
    UI4 --> L2
    L1 --> D4
    L1 --> D3
    L2 --> D1
    L2 --> D2
    L3 --> D4
    L4 --> D4

    L1 -.validates.-> L2
    L2 -.caches.-> D2
    L3 -.triggers.-> UI2
```

### 4.3 Release Versioning Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V100[v1.0.0 - Initial Launch] --> V110[v1.1.0 - Feature Add: Dark Mode]
    V100 --> V101[v1.0.1 - Patch: Login Bug Fix]
    V110 --> V111[v1.1.1 - Patch: Crash on Image Upload]
    V110 --> V120[v1.2.0 - Feature Add: Chat Module]
    V120 --> V200[v2.0.0 - Major: API v2 Migration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `V100`, `L1`) to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword safeguards. Labels contain only clean uppercase text.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024]**
**Define "Continuous Integration" in the context of mobile application deployment. List any two benefits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Continuous Integration (CI) is a software engineering practice where developers frequently merge their code changes into a shared central repository (e.g., Git), after which **automated builds and tests** are run to detect integration errors early.

**Benefits (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Early Bug Detection** — Integration issues are caught within minutes of commit, reducing the cost of fixing defects.
2. **Faster Release Cycles** — Automation eliminates manual build steps, enabling daily or hourly releases.
3. **Improved Code Quality** — Linting, static analysis, and test gates prevent broken code from reaching production.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024]**
**What is an Android App Bundle (AAB)? How is it different from an APK?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Android App Bundle (`.aab`)** is a publishing format introduced by Google that contains all of an app's compiled code, resources, and native libraries in a single artifact. Google Play then uses this bundle to generate and serve **optimized APKs** tailored to each user's device configuration (CPU architecture, screen density, language).

**Key Differences (any two):**
| Feature | APK | AAB |
|---|---|---|
| Format | Final installable package | Publishing format |
| Size | One-size-fits-all | Generates device-specific APKs |
| Dynamic Delivery | Not supported | Supported (on-demand modules) |
| Upload to Play Store | Legacy | **Mandatory since Aug 2021** |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **CI/CD pipeline** for a Flutter-based mobile application targeting both Android and iOS. List the key stages and the tools used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security hardening steps** that must be performed before deploying a production mobile app. Mention at least four techniques with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

A **CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) pipeline** for a Flutter app automates the journey from code commit to production release. The key stages are:

| Stage | Tools | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Source Control** | GitHub / GitLab / Bitbucket | Central repository, branch protection rules |
| **2. Trigger** | Webhook on `push` / `pull_request` | Initiates pipeline |
| **3. Static Analysis** | `flutter analyze`, `dartfmt`, SonarQube | Code quality gates |
| **4. Unit Testing** | `flutter test`, Mockito, Bloc Test | Validate business logic |
| **5. Build** | `flutter build apk --release`, `flutter build ios` | Generate platform binaries |
| **6. Code Signing** | Keystore (Android), Certs (iOS) | Cryptographic identity |
| **7. UI Testing** | Patrol, integration_test, BrowserStack | End-to-end validation |
| **8. Artifact Storage** | Firebase App Distribution, TestFlight, Play Console Internal | Distribute to testers |
| **9. Deployment** | Fastlane, Codemagic CLI, GitHub Actions | Push to store |
| **10. Monitoring** | Firebase Crashlytics, Sentry, Datadog | Post-release telemetry |

**Pipeline Flow (Logical):**
$$
\text{Commit} \xrightarrow{\text{webhook}} \text{Lint} \rightarrow \text{Test} \rightarrow \text{Build} \rightarrow \text{Sign} \rightarrow \text{Deploy}
$$

> Each stage has a **gate** — if the gate fails, the pipeline halts and notifies the developer. This prevents broken builds from reaching end users.

*(7 marks — full marks awarded for stage enumeration + tool mapping + logical flow diagram)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

The following four security hardening techniques are **mandatory** for production mobile apps under the KTU 2024 scheme's CO5 ("Apply security best practices in mobile applications"):

1. **Code Obfuscation (R8 / ProGuard)**
   - Renames classes, methods, and fields to meaningless symbols (e.g., `a`, `b`, `c`).
   - **Why:** Prevents reverse engineering and intellectual property theft.
   - **Configuration:** `minifyEnabled true` in `build.gradle`.

2. **Certificate Pinning**
   - Hardcodes the server's SSL certificate public key inside the app.
   - **Why:** Prevents Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks even if a rogue CA is installed on the device.
   - **Implementation:** Use `dio_certificate_pinning` (Flutter) or OkHttp's `CertificatePinner` (Android).

3. **Secure Storage of Secrets**
   - API keys, tokens, and user credentials must **never** be stored in `SharedPreferences` or `localStorage`.
   - **Use:** Android Keystore, iOS Keychain, or `flutter_secure_storage`.

4. **Network Security Configuration (HTTPS-Only)**
   - Disables cleartext HTTP traffic via `network_security_config.xml`.
   - **Why:** Prevents credential sniffing over unsecured Wi-Fi networks.

**Bonus technique (for 14/14):**
5. **Root / Jailbreak Detection** — Use `flutter_jailbreak_detection` or `RootBeer` to block app execution on compromised devices handling sensitive data (e.g., banking apps).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **staged rollout strategy** used by Google Play Store. Why is it preferred over a "big-bang" launch? Provide the mathematical expression for rollout percentage growth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Git Flow branching model**. How does it support the Milestone 4 integration phase? **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Staged Rollout** is a deployment strategy where a new app version is released to a **gradually increasing percentage** of the user base, rather than to all users at once.

**Typical Schedule (Google Play default):**
- Day 1: 1% → 2% → 5%
- Day 2: 10% → 20%
- Day 3: 50%
- Day 4: 100%

**Mathematical Expression:**
$$
R(t) = \min\left(100, R(t-1) \cdot k\right)
$$
Where $R(t)$ is the rollout percentage at time step $t$ and $k$ is the growth multiplier (typically $k = 2$).

**Why Preferred Over Big-Bang Launch:**

| Aspect | Big-Bang Launch | Staged Rollout |
|---|---|---|
| **Blast Radius of a Bug** | 100% of users affected | 1–5% only |
| **Rollback Speed** | Slow (re-submission) | Instant (Play Console halt) |
| **Crash Monitoring** | Overwhelmed by noise | Clean baseline comparison |
| **User Trust** | High risk | Low risk, controlled |

**Real-world Example:** When WhatsApp rolled out multi-device support, it used a 1% → 5% → 20% → 100% rollout to monitor battery drain issues.

*(7 marks — marks for math expression, comparison table, and real-world example)*

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Git Flow** is a branching model designed by Vincent Driessen (2010) that defines a strict branching structure around project releases.

**Branches and Their Roles:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[main - production ready]
    D[develop - integration branch]
    F1[feature or auth]
    F2[feature or payment]
    R1[release or v1.0.0]
    H1[hotfix or login crash]
    
    D --> F1
    D --> F2
    F1 --> D
    F2 --> D
    D --> R1
    R1 --> M
    M --> H1
    H1 --> M
    H1 --> D
```

| Branch | Purpose | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| `main` | Always deployable, tagged releases | Permanent |
| `develop` | Integration of all features | Permanent |
| `feature/*` | New functionality | Per-feature |
| `release/*` | Stabilization before launch | Short (days) |
| `hotfix/*` | Urgent production patches | Very short (hours) |

**How It Supports Milestone 4:**

1. **Parallel Development** — Multiple team members work on `feature/*` branches without blocking each other.
2. **Clean Integration Point** — `develop` is the single integration branch where CI runs regression tests.
3. **Isolated Stabilization** — `release/v1.0.0` allows final QA, bug fixes, and version bumping **without** injecting new features.
4. **Emergency Response** — `hotfix/*` branches let the team patch critical production bugs without waiting for the next release cycle.

**Practical Milestone 4 Workflow:**
- All features merged to `develop` → CI runs full test suite.
- Once green, create `release/v1.0.0` from `develop`.
- QA team tests, fixes merged back to `release/v1.0.0`.
- When stable, merge `release/v1.0.0` → `main` and tag `v1.0.0`.
- Deploy to Play Store.

*(7 marks — marks for diagram, branch table, and Milestone 4 mapping)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Milestone 4 Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing CI with CD** — CI = automated testing on commit; CD = automated deployment. Many students use them interchangeably. (**−1 mark**)
> 2. **Writing "I tested manually"** — Manual testing is acceptable but does **not** satisfy the 2024 Scheme CO4 ("Apply automated testing frameworks"). Always mention the testing tool. (**−2 marks**)
> 3. **Forgetting the version code** — `versionCode` must be a monotonically increasing **integer**, not a string. (**−1 mark**)
> 4. **Storing API keys in code** — Instant 50% mark deduction on security questions if `google_maps_api_key` is hardcoded in `MainActivity.kt`. (**−3 marks**)
> 5. **Skipping the rollback strategy** — Examiners specifically look for "what happens if the release crashes?" Always mention staged rollout + Crashlytics.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Milestone 4:**

- ✅ **Milestone 4 = Integration + Testing + Optimization + Deployment** (in that order).
- ✅ **CI/CD tools** to memorize: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Codemagic, Fastlane, Firebase App Distribution, TestFlight.
- ✅ **AAB > APK** — Google Play now mandates App Bundles.
- ✅ **Version Code** = integer, **Version Name** = semantic string (`MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`).
- ✅ **Security triad:** Obfuscation (R8) + Certificate Pinning + Secure Storage.
- ✅ **Staged Rollout** formula: $R(t) = \min(100, R(t-1) \cdot k)$ with $k=2$.
- ✅ **Android Vitals thresholds:** Crash-free users $\ge 99.5\%$, ANR rate $\le 0.47\%$, cold start $\le 2\text{s}$.
- ✅ **Git Flow branches:** `main`, `develop`, `feature/*`, `release/*`, `hotfix/*`.
- ✅ **Mandatory deliverables** for KTU submission: Signed AAB/IPA, test report, deployment screenshots, Postman/API docs, README with setup steps.
- ✅ **Post-deployment:** Always integrate **Crashlytics** for real-time crash reporting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
